title: Onboard existing project
description:
## Welcome to SlyCode

This card guides an AI-assisted onboarding of your existing project into SlyCode. Here's what to expect:

- The AI will scan your codebase **(read-only at first)**, understand what's there, and set up your kanban board with discovered work items
- **No files will be modified** until the implementation phase, and you'll approve all changes before they happen
- By default, bypass permissions is enabled — the AI can read and modify files without prompting. If you want more control during onboarding, you can turn this off before starting

**To begin:** Click the **Terminal** tab above, then run the **Onboard** action from the footer toolbar. This will start an AI session that walks you through the process step by step.

---

## AI Instructions

> **Important rules:**
> - Do NOT modify this card's contents at any point (except saving the exclusion list as a note during onboard)
> - This is a scripted onboarding process — override the standard design/deep-design action behavior and follow these instructions instead
> - When in doubt about any action, defer to the user
> - The scaffold setup varies per user (may have skills, may not, may have partial setup) — handle each scenario gracefully

### ONBOARD PHASE (backlog → design)

1. **Explain** what this process will do in brief terms
2. **Sensitive files check**: Ask the user if there are any sensitive or confidential files or directories that should not be accessed or touched during this process. Reassure them: no project files will be modified during the design phase — this is read-only fact-finding only
3. **Git safety check**:
   - Check if the project is under source control
   - Check for uncommitted changes
   - If there are large uncommitted changes, show a `git status` summary and suggest a simple, transparent commit as a safety checkpoint (e.g. `git add -A && git commit -m "Pre-SlyCode onboarding checkpoint"`)
   - Show the user what would be committed and let them confirm before proceeding
   - If the user declines, proceed anyway — it's a recommendation, not a requirement
4. **Save exclusions**: If the user specified any excluded files/directories, save them as a note on this card (this is the ONE permitted card modification)
5. **Move to design**

### DESIGN PHASE

Override standard design behavior — follow this scripted process.

**Step 1: Initial AI configuration scan**
- Start by inspecting CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, GEMINI.md and similar AI config files
- Determine the project's AI posture:
  - **AI-enabled**: Real user-written content in AI config files → learn from them, don't plan to overwrite
  - **Scaffolded-default**: Our template AI config files (detectable by reading contents) → note that implementation should populate with real context
  - **Traditional**: No AI config files existed before scaffold → note that they were created by scaffold and need populating
- This initial scan informs the rest of the process

**Step 2: Launch specific sub-agents in parallel for fact-finding**
- Do a quick initial scan to decide which agents are relevant. Err on the side of launching them — agents that find nothing relevant can exit early
- Respect the exclusion list throughout all agent work

Specific agents to launch:
1. **Documentation agent**: Find all docs, READMEs, wikis, design documents, API docs. Note their locations and purposes.
2. **Source structure agent**: Map directory structure, identify key entry points, frameworks, architecture patterns, languages used.
3. **Feature discovery agent**: Find feature specs, numbered features (e.g. `features/` directory), completed work items. Map each to a potential "done" or "testing" card depending on completion status.
4. **Work-in-progress agent**: Find TODOs, active branches, partial implementations, in-progress work. Map to potential "implementation" cards.
5. **Backlog discovery agent**: Find backlog files, issue references, planned-but-not-started work, TODO comments flagged as future work. Map to potential "backlog" cards.
6. **Git agent**: Check git status, branch structure, remote configuration, .gitignore contents. Flag any issues (e.g. secrets in repo, missing ignores for the stack).

**Step 3: Synthesize findings into a design document**
- Create a design document capturing everything discovered about the project
- **Link the design document to this card** using `sly-kanban update <card-id> --design-ref "<path>"`
- Link discovered documentation to proposed cards where applicable (design-ref, feature-ref)
- Conduct back-and-forth with the user as needed to clarify findings
- Note the CLAUDE.md status and what implementation should do with it

**Step 4: Propose implementation plan**
- List all cards to be created with their proposed stages (done, testing, implementation, backlog)
- Describe planned CLAUDE.md updates (if template, what context to populate)
- Describe context-priming recommendation
- End with suggestion to move to implementation (or create a feature doc if the scope warrants it)

### IMPLEMENTATION PHASE

1. **Create cards**: Create all agreed kanban cards at appropriate stages
2. **Link docs**: Link any existing documentation to cards (design-ref, feature-ref, test-ref)
3. **CLAUDE.md** (if our default template or needs updating):
   - Check existing content first
   - Propose specific changes to the user
   - Get explicit approval before writing
   - Populate with discovered project context (purpose, stack, key files, conventions, architecture)
4. **Context priming**:
   - If the context-priming skill exists but has no areas configured → explain what it does in plain terms ("creates reference files that help AI understand your codebase structure, key patterns, and conventions"), offer to run it. User decides.
   - If areas exist but look stale or unrelated to the project → flag to user, discuss whether to keep, update, or rebuild
   - If areas exist and look correct → leave them alone
5. **Audit trail**: Log detailed notes on this card of everything done throughout the session
6. **Checklist**: Create a checklist on this card of every action taken, tick each one off — the user should be able to see 100% of what was changed
7. **Create follow-up card**: Auto-create an "Explore roadmap" card in backlog. After creating it, reorder it to the top of the backlog using `sly-kanban reorder backlog --top <card-id>`. The card description should be:

   > **This card helps capture your project's direction, vision, and priorities.** It builds on what was discovered during onboarding and fills in the bigger picture — where the project is going, what matters most, and what to build next. Onboard this card when you're ready.
   >
   > ---
   >
   > **AI Instructions:**
   > Override standard design behavior. This is a conversational, lightweight session — not a questionnaire. Guide the discussion naturally, capture what's useful, don't force answers for things the user hasn't thought about yet.
   >
   > **Design phase** — explore: project vision, current priorities, upcoming features, pain points, technical debt, dependencies/blockers, user/stakeholder context. Output: roadmap design doc, additional backlog cards, proposed CLAUDE.md updates (check existing content, get approval before writing).
   >
   > **Implementation phase** — apply agreed CLAUDE.md updates, create additional backlog cards, log notes and checklist.

8. **Suggest next steps**:
   - Run the `organise-backlog` action to review and reorder the populated board
   - Onboard the "Explore roadmap" card to capture project vision and direction
